title: Knot rolls
subtitle: White bread rolls shaped into tied knots
excerpt: A straightforward white bread dough shaped into long ropes and tied into simple overhand knots before proving. The knot creates a layered crumb and a pulled-apart surface texture that plain round rolls don't have.
sourceNotes: Knotted bread rolls are documented in European baking traditions from at least the medieval period. Knot-shaped bread (such as the pretzel form) appears in manuscripts from the sixth and seventh centuries CE. The simple overhand knot roll for domestic baking is described in British domestic cookery guides from the Victorian period and is a standard shape in both British and Italian (rosetta-adjacent) baking traditions.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Reheat from frozen at 200°C for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape and chill overnight on a tray after knotting. Prove at room temperature for 1 hour before baking.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: 220°C conventional / 200°C fan for the first 10 minutes, then 200°C / 180°C fan for the remaining time

content.text: Knot rolls use a slightly higher hydration than typical dinner rolls (65%) to produce an open crumb rather than a pillowy one. The dough is smooth and workable but not tight. Each piece is rolled into a rope and tied in a simple overhand knot. During proving and baking, the knot opens slightly, giving the roll its textured top. The higher starting oven temperature gives a good initial spring and colour before the heat drops to finish the bake.
Mix, knead, and bulk ferment, 1 hour

Combine the {{strong-white-flour}}, {{fast-action-yeast}}, {{fine-salt}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Add the {{whole-milk}}, {{water}}, and {{unsalted-butter}} and mix to a rough dough. Knead by hand for 10 minutes or in a stand mixer for 6-7 minutes until smooth and elastic with a slight tack. The 65% hydration dough should clear the sides of the bowl but stick slightly to the base. Cover and leave to bulk ferment until doubled, about 1 hour.
Shape into knots and prove, 45 minutes

Line two baking trays with baking paper. Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces (about 75 g each). Roll each piece into a rope about 25 cm long by rolling under your palms on an unfloured surface. Tie each rope in a simple overhand knot and tuck the ends under. Place on the trays with plenty of space between them. Cover with oiled cling film and prove until puffy and noticeably larger, about 45 minutes.

Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Brush the rolls with milk or water. Bake for 10 minutes at 220°C, then reduce to 200°C (180°C fan) and bake for a further 8-10 minutes until deep golden. The internal temperature should read 94-96°C on a probe thermometer. Cool on a rack for at least 15 minutes before serving.
